Primary School · Fake News Project

Real or Fake News?

Read each animal news story carefully. Can you spot which ones are fake?

🔍 How to spot fake news

Before reading the news stories, learn to recognise the warning signs. Fake news uses clever tricks to fool us!

🚨

Alarming or sensational headlines

Fake news uses scary, exaggerated titles designed to grab your attention at all costs. If a headline seems impossible or makes you feel very scared, be suspicious.

🖼️

Suspicious images

The photos are often montages or blurry, unprofessional images. Sometimes they are stolen photos from a completely different story. You can check by searching the image on Google Images.

📋

No sources or dates

Fake news doesn't say who wrote it, when it happened, or where the information comes from. Real news always includes the journalist's name, the date, and the original source.

📤

Asking you to share it

If a story says "forward this to all your contacts" or "something bad will happen if you don't share it," that is a very clear warning sign. Real news never does that.

🔗

Fake-looking links and names

Sometimes they use web addresses very similar to trusted news sites, like bbc-news-today24.com instead of bbc.co.uk. Always read the address carefully before trusting it!

💡

Remember: if you see any of these signs, do not trust the story straight away. Look it up in a reliable newspaper, ask a trusted adult, or check fact-checking websites like FullFact.org or Snopes.com.

0 / 4 stories
✓ TRUE
✗ FAKE
National Geographic · 14 March 2023

African elephants remember water sources visited more than 30 years ago, according to the Zoological Society of London

Researchers at the Zoological Society of London have published a study showing that female leader elephants can remember routes to water sources they used in their youth, more than three decades ago. The finding was observed in Amboseli National Park (Kenya) during a severe drought, when herds travelled towards water that none of the younger animals had ever visited. Scientists believe this extraordinary memory is key to the species' survival in the face of climate change.

Published in: National Geographic
✅ Verified by: FullFact.org — Information confirmed
🤔 Do you think this news story is true or fake?
✅ It's TRUE! Elephants have one of the most remarkable memories in the animal kingdom. The oldest females remember water routes they haven't travelled in decades, leading their entire herd to safety during extreme droughts.
🔗 Read on National Geographic →
✓ TRUE
✗ FAKE
ocean-news-facts24.blogspot.com · 2024

Great white sharks have learned to fear mobile phones, according to researchers — scientists recommend playing reggaeton to stay safe at the beach

A revolutionary study by the Florida Marine Institute (no address given) claims that great white sharks have developed an instinctive fear of the vibrations from mobile phones. According to the document —which has no author or date— swimmers only need to bring a switched-on phone into the water to be completely protected. Experts recommend playing reggaeton music as it is the "most repellent frequency." The article ends: "Share this with ALL your contacts before going to the beach this summer."

Published on: ocean-news-facts24.blogspot.com
🔍 Debunked by: Snopes.com — False information
🤔 Do you think this news story is true or fake?
❌ It's FAKE! There are multiple clear warning signs: the headline uses capital letters and alarming punctuation; the source is an unknown blog with no date or author; the content is absurd (reggaeton does not repel sharks 😄); and at the end it asks you to share it with everyone. No serious scientific study has ever proven this.
🔍 Check Snopes.com →
✓ TRUE
✗ FAKE
BBC Science · 8 June 2022

Sea turtles travel thousands of kilometres using the Earth's magnetic field as a GPS to return to the exact beach where they were born

A team of marine biologists from the University of North Carolina (USA) has confirmed that loggerhead and leatherback sea turtles can detect the unique "magnetic signature" of the beach where they hatched and return to it decades later to lay their own eggs. The phenomenon, known as philopatry, has been documented using satellite tracking equipment over more than 20 years. The researchers warn that light and plastic pollution on beaches can disrupt their navigation system and put the species at risk.

Published in: BBC Science
✅ Verified by: WWF — Information confirmed
🤔 Do you think this news story is true or fake?
✅ It's TRUE! This amazing instinct is called philopatry. Sea turtles detect the unique magnetic signal of their home beach and return there decades later. It is one of the most fascinating phenomena in marine biology.
🔗 More info at WWF →
✓ TRUE
✗ FAKE
zoo-amazing-facts.net.es.tl · 2024

Scientists decode the secret language of Madrid Zoo lions: they talk about their holidays and share opinions about their food

Linguists from the "Special Animal University of Madrid" (unknown institution) claim to have fully decoded the language of the zoo's lions. According to the study (no author names given), the roars are complete sentences in which the lions discuss their weekend plans and criticise the quality of their food. The zoo director —whose name does not appear— confirms that "very soon" translation headsets will be available for visitors. The article ends by asking: "Spread this news so that everyone knows about it!"

Published on: zoo-amazing-facts.net.es.tl
🔍 Debunked by: FullFact.org — False information
🤔 Do you think this news story is true or fake?
❌ It's FAKE! The warning signs are everywhere: an exaggerated headline with capital letters ("INCREDIBLE", "SECRET LANGUAGE"), a made-up institution, no date or real authors, absurd content, and a request to spread the story. Lions do communicate with roars, but they do not have a human-like language.
🔍 Check FullFact.org →

✏️ Open Questions Reflection

🔵 Multiple Choice Self-marking

A) It is published in a national newspaper with a date and an author
B) It has an exaggerated headline and asks you to forward it to your contacts
C) It includes the name of the scientist who did the research
D) It has been published by three different news outlets
A) Share it quickly so everyone finds out
B) Check if it seems exaggerated and look for the same story in other sources
C) Post it only if you find it very funny
D) Wait to see how many likes it gets

Sea turtles use the Earth's to navigate and return to the beach where they were born.
